Name: spectral_precision_routing
Title: Spectral Precision Routing: Training-Free Precision Allocation via Activation Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling dynamic precision allocation without retraining.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Spectral Latent Steering have explored the use of spectral methods for precision allocation and test-time optimization. However, our proposal differs in its focus on training-free precision allocation via activation geometry, and its use of a curvature-aware precision routing framework.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Precision Routing, a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. Our approach computes local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates of hidden states, mapping them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed Spectral Precision Routing framework on WILDS benchmarks using single-GPU setups.
Compare the performance of our method with static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Investigate the effect of different precision allocation strategies on the throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier.
Analyze the computational overhead of our approach and its impact on latency budgets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may require careful tuning of hyperparameters to achieve optimal performance.
The use of spectral methods may be sensitive to the choice of activation functions and network architectures.
The framework may not be suitable for applications with extremely tight latency budgets or very large models.
